summary: This Mutual Termination Agreement ends the Domain Name License Agreement that Beijing SINA and Shanghai SINA Leju had signed on May 8, 2008. By signing, both companies agree that, as of the execution date, the original contract is completely terminated and none of its terms continue to apply. The agreement is the entire understanding between the parties and can only be changed in writing signed by both sides. It is governed by Chinese law, can be signed in multiple copies, and binds each party and their successors. The main risk is that once signed, the original licensing rights and obligations cease, so any reliance on those rights after the termination date could lead to disputes or loss of benefits.
termination_clause: 1. Beijing SINA and SINA Leju agree that, upon the date of execution of this Termination Agreement, the Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force or effect, and, for the avoidance of doubt, no provisions of the Original Agreement survive such termination.

summary: This Reseller Agreement lets Worldwide Strategies sell TouchStar’s call‑center software and support services in a defined territory on a non‑exclusive basis. TouchStar grants the reseller the right to market, distribute, and license the software, while retaining the ability to work with other customers and appoint additional distributors. The reseller must follow all legal requirements, obtain TouchStar’s written approval before using any third‑party "Other Resellers," and ensure those parties comply with the agreement. The reseller is financially responsible for any unauthorized or illegal use of the software by its sub‑resellers, including reverse‑engineering or violations of export controls. Failure to meet these obligations could result in liability for damages and termination of the relationship.
liability_clause: Reseller shall be responsible for all actions of Other Resellers with regard to the promotion, marketing, distribution, licensing and sale of TouchStar Software. Reseller shall be liable for any unauthorized or illegal use of the TouchStar Software by any Other Reseller, including, but not limited to, any actions or attempts to Reverse Engineer the TouchStar Software and any promotion, marketing, distribution, licensing or sale of the TouchStar Software in violation of Government Controls or other Legal Requirements.

summary: This Sponsorship Agreement sets out a relationship where Domini Social Investments LLC (the Sponsor) provides oversight, administrative and management services to the Domini Advisor Trust (the Trust). The Sponsor must maintain office facilities, supervise trustees, handle compliance filings, prepare meeting materials, and answer public inquiries, while the Trust pays all operating expenses and a fee of 0.50% of its average daily net assets to the Sponsor. The Sponsor’s liability is limited except for willful misconduct, bad faith, or gross negligence. Either party can end the agreement with at least 60 days’ written notice and no penalty. Risks include the Sponsor’s limited liability and the Trust’s responsibility for all expenses, which could be substantial.
termination_clause:
6. Duration, Termination and Amendments of this Agreement. This Agreement shall become effective as of the day and year first above written and shall govern the relations between the parties hereto thereafter, unless terminated as set forth in this Section 6.

This Agreement may not be altered or amended, except by an instrument in writing and executed by both parties. This Agreement may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, with respect to any series or the Trust, by the Board of Trustees of the Trust, or by the Sponsor, in each case on not less than 60 days' written notice to the other party.
liability_clause: 4. Limitation of Liability of the Sponsor. The Sponsor shall not be liable for any error of judgment or mistake of law or for any act or omission in the oversight, administration or management of the Trust or the performance of its duties hereunder, except for willful misfeasance, bad faith or gross negligence in the performance of its duties, or by reason of the reckless disregard of its obligations and duties hereunder. As used in this Section 4, the term "Sponsor" shall include Domini and/or any of its affiliates and the directors, officers and employees of Domini and/or any of its affiliates.

summary: This Distributor Agreement lets WatchGuard Technologies (WGT) appoint European Micro as a non‑exclusive reseller of its hardware‑software product package in a defined territory. WGT supplies the products at discounted prices and provides training and support; the distributor must meet guaranteed minimum purchase levels, keep inventory, market the products, provide reseller support, and report sales data. Either party can end the agreement for cause—such as breach of obligations, insolvency, or unauthorized assignments—or, after the initial term, with notice and a termination fee equal to 50% of any unpaid guaranteed purchases. Risks include the distributor’s liability for failing to meet purchase minimums (triggering liquidated damages) and the loss of rights to market and use WGT’s trademarks upon termination.
termination_clause:
Section 5. Term and Termination.
5.1 Term. ...
5.2 Termination by WGT. Upon the occurrence of any of the following, WGT may terminate the Term by giving Distributor written notice of such termination for: (a) any failure of Distributor to comply with the marketing requirements under paragraph 4.1(b); (b) any material change in the general management, ownership or control of Distributor... (f) any failure by Distributor to perform any of its other obligations under this Agreement where such failure continues for thirty (30) days after written notice thereof by WGT to Distributor; or (g) WGT giving Distributor ninety (90) days' advance written notice of termination at any time after the expiration of the Initial Term.
5.3 Failure to Make Guaranteed Minimum Purchases. ... WGT may, at its sole option and effective upon notice to Distributor, terminate this Agreement. Distributor shall pay WGT fifty percent (50%) of the unpaid balance of cumulative Guaranteed Minimum Purchase amounts as liquidated damages.
5.4 Termination by Distributor. Upon the occurrence of any of the following, Distributor may terminate the Term by giving WGT written notice of such termination; (a) the insolvency of WGT... (b) any failure by WGT to perform any of its obligations... (c) for convenience ... Distributor pays to WGT a lump‑sum payment equal to fifty (50%) of the unpaid balance of cumulative Guaranteed Minimum Purchase amounts.
5.5 Effect of Termination. ... Distributor will: (a) immediately cease to demonstrate, market, sublicense and distribute the Product in the Territory; (b) cease use of all Trademarks of ...

summary: FAILED

contract_id: SLOVAKWIRELESSFINANCECOBV_03_28_2001-EX-4.(B)(II).3-Maintenance and support contract for SICAP(R) modules
summary: This Maintenance and Support Agreement sets out the relationship between EuroTel (the customer) and Sicap Ltd (the service provider) for the SICAP® PPB, VCA, and OTA software modules. Sicap Ltd must provide a standard hotline, up to 20 hours of telephone support per month, remote assistance, and change‑management services, while EuroTel is responsible for first‑level fault tracing, training its staff, and supplying necessary logs and system access. EuroTel may request additional remote or on‑site support, which will be billed separately. Risks include extra charges for misuse, third‑party software bugs, and any support outside normal working hours. If Sicap Ltd cannot resolve an emergency within the stipulated time, it must dispatch staff on the next available flight or train, and EuroTel’s personnel must be on‑site to follow instructions.

This Gas Franchise Agreement lets the Town of Vinton give Roanoke Gas Company the right to build, operate, and maintain a gas distribution system in the town for 20 years starting Jan. 1, 2016. In return, the gas company must pay a franchise fee that is a share of a $98,196 base fee, calculated each year from its gas sales in Vinton and two neighboring cities, and the fee must be paid by March 31 of the following year. The company also must keep a staffed business office in Vinton at least 40 hours a week, follow nondiscrimination rules, and send any required notices by certified mail or courier. Failure to pay the fee on time, keep the office open, or comply with the nondiscrimination requirement could constitute a breach and expose the company to penalties or loss of the franchise.

This Manufacturing and Supply Agreement sets out the relationship between Pfizer (the Manufacturer) and Upjohn (the Customer) after their corporate separation. Pfizer agrees to produce and deliver pharmaceutical products to Upjohn for sale in the defined territory, while Upjohn commits to purchase those products, provide forecasts, and pay the agreed prices. Both parties must follow strict manufacturing, quality, environmental, and regulatory standards, maintain records, and protect each other's confidential information. Risks include potential termination for cause, breach of anti‑bribery rules, or insolvency, which could disrupt supply and trigger penalties. Liability is limited by the agreement’s indemnification and limitation provisions, meaning each side’s exposure to damages is capped and subject to insurance. Non‑compliance with quality or regulatory requirements could lead to product rejection, disposal costs, or legal liability.

This Consulting Agreement sets out the relationship between Immunotolerance, Inc. and consultant Alan Crane. Crane will provide advisory services and, in return, receive a large block of company stock and reimbursement for approved expenses, but no employee benefits. The Company can end the agreement at any time for cause—such as repeated failure to perform, dishonesty, or criminal conviction—and must pay for services rendered up to termination; Crane can quit with 30 days' notice. Crane must keep all proprietary and confidential information secret, return all company materials, and assign any inventions related to the company's business to the Company. Risks include loss of the consulting role if cause is found, strict non‑disclosure obligations, and the assignment of potentially valuable inventions to the Company.
termination_clause: 4. Termination. The Company may terminate the Consultation Period at any time for Cause (as defined below). In the event of such termination, the Consultant shall be entitled to payment hereunder and for expenses paid or incurred prior to the effective date of termination. Such payments shall constitute full settlement of any and all claims of the Consultant of every description against the Company. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Company may terminate the Consultation Period effective immediately upon receipt of written notice by the Consultant, if the Consultant breaches or threatens to breach any provision of Section 6. For purposes of this Section 4, "Cause" shall exist in the event of (i) a good faith finding by the Board of Directors of the Company (A) of repeated and willful failure of the Consultant after written notice to perform his reasonable Services for the Company, or (B) that the Consultant has engaged in dishonesty, gross negligence or misconduct; or (ii) the conviction of the Consultant of, or the entry of a pleading of guilty or nolo contendere by the Consultant to, any crime involving moral turpitude or any felony. The Consultant may terminate the Consultation Period at any time upon thirty (30) days' written notice.
confidentiality_clause:
6.1 Proprietary Information.

(a) The Consultant acknowledges that Consultant's relationship with the Company is one of high trust and confidence and that in the course of Consultant's service to the Company, Consultant will have access to and contact with Proprietary Information. The Consultant will not disclose any Proprietary Information to any person or entity other than employees of the Company or use the same for any purposes (other than in the performance of the services) without written approval by an officer of the Company, either during or after the Consultation Period, unless and until such Proprietary Information has become public knowledge without fault by the Consultant.

(b) For purposes of this Agreement, Proprietary Information shall mean, by way of illustration and not limitation, all information, whether or not in writing, whether or not patentable and whether or not copyrightable, of a private, secret or confidential nature, owned, possessed or used by the Company, concerning the Company's business, business relationships or financial affairs, including, without limitation, any Invention, formula, vendor information, customer information, apparatus, equipment, trade secret, process, research, report, technical or research data, clinical data, know-how, computer program, software, software documentation, hardware design, technology, product, processes, methods, techniques, formulas, compounds, projects, developments, marketing or business plan, forecast, unpublished financial statement, budget, license, price, cost, customer, supplier or personnel information or employee list that is communicated to, learned of, developed or otherwise acquired by the Consultant in the course of Consultant's service as a consultant to the Company.

(c) The Consultant's obligations under this Section 6.1 shall not apply to any information that (i) is or becomes known to the general public under circumstances involving no breach by the Consultant or others of the terms of this Section 6.1, (ii) is generally disclosed to third parties by the Company without restriction on such third parties, or (iii) is approved for release by written authorization of an officer of the Company.

(d) The Consultant agrees that all files, documents, letters, memoranda, reports, records, data, sketches, drawings, models, laboratory notebooks, program listings, computer equipment or devices, computer programs or other written, photographic, or other tangible material containing Proprietary Information, whether created by the Consultant or others, which shall come into Consultant's custody or possession, shall be and are the exclusive property of the Company to be used by the Consultant only in the performance of Consultant's duties for the Company and shall not be copied or removed from the Company premises except in the pursuit of the business of the Company. All such materials or copies thereof and all tangible property of the Company in the custody or possession of the Consultant shall be delivered to the Company, upon the earlier of (i) a request by the Company or (ii) the termination of this Agreement. After such delivery, the Consultant shall not retain any such materials or copies thereof or any such tangible property.

(e) The Consultant agrees that Consultant's obligation not to disclose or to use information and materials of the types set forth in paragraphs (b) and (d) above, and Consultant's obligation to return materials and tangible property set forth in paragraph (d) above extends to such types of information, materials and tangible property of customers of the Company or suppliers to the Company or other third parties who may have disclosed or entrusted the same to the Company or to the Consultant.

(f) The Consultant acknowledges that the Company from time to time may have agreements with other persons or with the United States Government, or agencies thereof, that impose obligations or restrictions on the Company regarding inventions made during the course of work under such agreements or regarding the confidential nature of such work. The Consultant agrees to be bound by all such obligations and restrictions that are known to Consultant and to take all action necessary to discharge the obligations of the Company under such agreements.

summary: This agreement creates a five‑year, renewable partnership between T&B Seminars (owner of Tarek El Moussa’s name, image and related intellectual property) and Legacy Education Alliance (LEA) to develop and run a branded real‑estate education business. T&B grants LEA an exclusive worldwide license to use its intellectual property to create educational materials and run seminars, while LEA handles marketing, event planning, sales, and delivery of the products. Both parties must keep each other's confidential information private and share any improvements to the IP. T&B can terminate early if LEA fails to meet sales benchmarks, and either side can end the contract with 90‑day notice at the end of each five‑year term. Risks include loss of the license if sales targets aren’t met and potential disputes over ownership of jointly created work product.
termination_clause: The term "Term" shall mean an initial term of five years, automatically renewable thereafter for successive 5-year terms unless either party provides prior written notice of termination not less than 90 days prior to the end of such five-year term; provided, however, T&B shall have the right to terminate the license after the first year of the Term if LEA does not conduct the Business so as to meet the Cash Sales benchmarks set by the parties for years two through five of the Term, as set forth in Section 3.3, below.
confidentiality_clause: The term "Confidential Information" means any and all information which is not readily ascertainable by proper means and which derives economic value, actual or potential from not being generally known and which has been the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. All information relating to the products or operations of a party, which is provided to the other party, or to which the other party otherwise obtains access, pursuant to, or as a result of, this Agreement shall be treated as Confidential Information hereunder, except such information which the other party can clearly show: (a) at the time of this Agreement is publicly and openly known; (b) after the date of this Agreement becomes publicly and openly known through no fault of the other party; (c) comes into the other party's possession and lawfully obtained by the other party from a source other than from the party or a source deriving from the party, and not subject to any obligation of confidentiality or restrictions on use; or (d) is approved for release by written authorization of the other party

summary: This Sponsorship Agreement lets Tickets.com become MP3.com’s exclusive source for ticket sales, with MP3.com displaying a small Tickets portal on several music genre pages. MP3.com must deliver at least 3 million portal impressions each month, and Tickets must pay a series of scheduled sponsor fees, with a 1.5% monthly late‑payment charge. Tickets can end the deal with 30‑day notice before the 60‑day mark, and may renew for a second year under limited fee increases. Both parties warrant their sites won’t infringe rights or contain illegal content and must indemnify each other for breaches. Liability is limited: neither side is responsible for special or consequential damages, except for indemnification claims. Risks include missed impression targets, late payments, and potential disputes over impression counts.
termination_clause: This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall remain in full force and effect until one (1) year subsequent to the Effective Date, provided however, that Tickets may terminate this Agreement for any reason upon thirty (30) days' notice to MP3. com at any time prior to the expiration of sixty (60) days subsequent to the Effective Date. Furthermore, for a thirty (30) day period, beginning thirty (30) days prior to the first anniversary of this Agreement, Tickets shall have the right to renew the Agreement for another year with Sponsor Fees that do not exceed a [***] percent increase over the existing Sponsor Fees. Any payments which have accrued prior to the date of termination shall remain due and payable. Sections 6, 7, and 8 shall survive termination of this agreement.
liability_clause: No Consequential Damages. Except for claims arising under section 6, in no event will either party be liable for any special, indirect, incidental or consequential damages.

summary: This Intellectual Property Agreement, dated December 31, 2018, governs the transfer and licensing of intellectual property between Arizona (the seller/licensor) and the Company (the buyer/licensee) in connection with a prior Stock Purchase Agreement. Arizona assigns all of its "Arizona Assigned IP" to the Company and must cooperate to record and perfect that ownership. In return, each party grants the other a perpetual, royalty‑free, non‑exclusive license to use the other’s licensed copyrights, know‑how, patents and certain trademarks in the opposite party’s field of business. Trademark licenses are limited in time (24 months for Arizona‑licensed marks, 18 months for Diamond marks) and are non‑sublicensable and non‑assignable, with strict quality‑control requirements to protect goodwill. Risks include breaching quality‑control standards, which could damage the reputation of the trademarks and trigger remedial actions, and the limited term of trademark use, which may require renegotiation.

This Reseller Agreement lets Diversinet grant the Reseller an exclusive right to market and sell Diversinet’s security products in Canada, provided the Reseller meets an annual minimum purchase commitment and follows strict marketing, branding, and reporting rules. Diversinet must deliver the products on time and provide documentation, while the Reseller must obtain customer agreements, pay all invoices within 30 days, and protect Diversinet’s trademarks. The agreement runs for five years and auto‑renews yearly unless either side gives notice. Either party can terminate for non‑payment, material breach, or if the Reseller stops operating, with a 30‑day notice (or 60 days for breach cure). Upon termination, the Reseller must cease using the products, pay any outstanding amounts, destroy all product copies, and certify compliance. Risks include loss of exclusive rights, hefty late‑payment interest, and a mandatory 18‑month phase‑out period.
termination_clause:
4. Termination (a) Either Party may terminate this Agreement: (i) Upon thirty (30) days' written notice to Reseller if Reseller fails to make any AMC payment when due under this Agreement, or when an audit, as set out in Section 13, by Diversinet reveals any under-payment for any payment due, and it is not paid within the thirty (30) day written notice period; (ii) providing thirty (60) days' written notice to the other Party if the other Party breaches any material term or condition of this Agreement, and fails to cure such breach within the 60 day period; (iii) upon providing thirty (30) days' written notice to Reseller if Reseller terminates the provisions of the Annual Minimum Commitment and fails to meet the sales targets as set out in Schedule 3. (b) This Agreement shall terminate automatically without notice and without further action by Diversinet in the event that:

(i) Reseller ceases carrying on its business, or if a petition shall be filed, an order shall be made or an effective resolution is passed for the winding-up, dissolution or liquidation of Reseller. (c) Termination under Subsection 4(a) shall trigger a phase-out period during which Reseller may continue to provide products and services to Customers. Such phase-out period shall be the longer of (i) the remaining term of the agreement between the Reseller and its Customers, and (ii) 18 months. (d) Upon the termination of this Agreement for any reason: (1) all rights granted to Reseller hereunder shall automatically come to an end and revert to Diversinet, including without limitation any right to use, operate and provide customer service or support for the Products; (2) Reseller shall immediately pay to Diversinet all amounts due and outstanding hereunder as of the date of such termination or expiration; and (3) within five (5) days of termination of this Agreement, for whatever reason, Reseller, must: (a) destroy all copies of the Products and any associated Products documentation in its possession or under its direct control; and (b) deliver to Diversinet a written certificate warranting that it has complied with the foregoing obligations.

summary: This Strategic Alliance Agreement sets up a partnership between Freedom Mortgage and its new affiliate Cherry Hill. Freedom Mortgage will originate and service mortgage loans, while Cherry Hill will provide capital and eventually operate as a real‑estate investment trust. The parties must execute several ancillary agreements—including an Acknowledgement Agreement with Ginnie Mae, a Purchase Agreement for excess mortgage‑servicing rights, and a Flow Agreement for loan sales—before the closing date. Freedom Mortgage is responsible for obtaining any needed permits, maintaining its financial thresholds, and covering costs related to designating a standby issuer if trigger events occur. Cherry Hill must cooperate with revisions to the Acknowledgement Agreement and will be indemnified against losses if Ginnie Mae terminates Freedom Mortgage’s issuer status. Risks include potential breach of financial covenants, trigger events that could force standby arrangements, and the need to meet regulatory approvals.

This agreement lets Companion Healthcare Technologies (CHT) act as the exclusive reseller of Ehave's software platform for managing companion animal health. Ehave grants CHT a non‑transferable right to license the platform to its own customers (End Users) and their authorized users, but only within the defined field of use and territory. Both parties must follow the detailed definitions, schedules, and security requirements set out in the contract, and the agreement is governed by Ontario law. Key risks include the lack of expressly stated termination, confidentiality, and liability provisions in the excerpt, meaning the parties may rely on default legal rules or other sections of the full contract to address how the relationship can end, how sensitive information is protected, and what damages each side may be responsible for.

This Distributorship Agreement lets Zogenix, the maker of the drug Fintepla®, appoint Nippon Shinyaku as its exclusive distributor in Japan for treating Dravet and Lennox‑Gastaut syndromes. Zogenix must manufacture and supply the product, while the Distributor must promote, market, sell, and handle the drug in the territory, following all regulatory requirements and using commercially reasonable efforts. Both parties must keep each other's confidential information private and comply with applicable laws, including anti‑bribery rules. The contract includes provisions for termination, limitation of liability, and indemnification, meaning either side could end the deal under certain conditions and may be liable only for direct damages, with indirect or punitive losses generally excluded. Breaches could trigger damages, loss of exclusivity, or other legal remedies.

This Master Supply Agreement sets out the framework for Reynolds Consumer Products (the Seller) to sell, and Pactiv (the Buyer) to purchase, specified consumer‑goods products for resale. The parties may create detailed Purchase Schedules that define product types, quantities, prices, and delivery terms. Each party must comply with all applicable laws, maintain ethical standards, and provide warranties—Seller guarantees product condition, compliance, and title; Buyer must handle products properly. The agreement can be ended by written notice after the last Purchase Schedule expires or as otherwise provided, and many obligations (e.g., confidentiality, breach remedies) survive termination. Risks include potential breach of warranties, the need to provide adequate performance assurances, and the obligation to indemnify the other party for third‑party claims arising from product defects or negligence.
termination_clause: 1. Term. The "Term" of this Agreement will commence on the Effective Date and will end on the earlier of: (a) the first anniversary of the expiration date of the last Purchase Schedule (as defined in this next Section); (b) a termination date elected by a Party in a written notice delivered to the other Party any time after the expiration of the last Purchase Schedule; or (c) a termination date elected by a Party in a written notice delivered to the other Party as provided in Subsection 11(d) of this Agreement. The rights and obligations of the Parties under this Agreement will survive the expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement with respect to any (i) products purchased and sold under this Agreement during the Term and products sold after the Term for orders accepted during the Term; (ii) Confidential Information (as defined in Section 10 of this Agreement) disclosed or received by a Party during the Term; (iii) breach of this Agreement by a Party; (iv) any other statement, decision, act or omission of a Party concerning or related to this Agreement; (v) any Dispute (as defined in Section 11 of this Agreement) between the Parties concerning or related to this Agreement; (vi) products and other materials manufactured or maintained by Seller in inventory for sale to Buyer that Buyer is obligated to purchase under a Purchase Schedule; and (vii) any provision that expressly states that it will survive the expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement.

summary: This endorsement updates an existing variable life insurance contract after Thrivent Life Insurance Company was dissolved and its assets transferred to Thrivent Financial for Lutherans. The purpose is to replace all references to the former company with the new society name and confirm that all rights, obligations, and benefits remain unchanged except for the listed amendments. Key obligations include the insurer’s duty to maintain solvency; if a solvency shortfall occurs, the policyholder must pay an extra amount within 60 days or have the amount charged to the contract with 5% annual interest. The policyholder may transfer ownership of the contract according to the society’s bylaws, but membership rights cannot be transferred. Risks for the policyholder involve potential extra payments and interest if the society’s solvency is impaired, while the insurer assumes responsibility for all contract obligations under its new name.

contract_id: Zounds Hearing, Inc. - MANUFACTURING DESIGN MARKETING AGREEMENT
summary: This Manufacturing, Design and Marketing Agreement lets Subcontractor (Zounds Hearing) provide design, engineering, manufacturing and FDA‑registered facility space to Manufacturer (InnerScope) so the Manufacturer can produce hearing‑aid products under its own brand. Subcontractor receives a $1,000,000 technology access fee in two installments and is paid for each product’s manufacturing cost plus agreed royalties (80% of cost or $80 per unit for non‑rechargeable, 100% of cost or $100 per unit for rechargeable). The Manufacturer must place non‑cancellable purchase orders, pay the full product cost up front, and cover shipping, taxes and any third‑party royalties. Risks include the Manufacturer’s obligation to pay even if cost‑reduction benefits are not shared, the non‑cancellable nature of orders, and the Manufacturer’s sole responsibility for warranty and liability for any product defects.

This Transportation Service Agreement sets out a ten‑year deal (with month‑to‑month renewal afterward) where Arkansas Western Pipeline Company (the Transporter) moves natural gas for Associated Natural Gas Company (the Shipper). The Shipper must deliver gas at a specified receipt point and the Transporter must transport an “Equivalent Quantity” to the delivery point, not exceeding a maximum daily quantity of 23,000 dekatherms. The Shipper pays rates per the Transporter's FTS/ITS tariff, which can be changed with regulatory approval. Risks include capacity reductions that may proportionally cut the Shipper’s quota, and the need to give 60‑ or 180‑day written notice to end the contract. No confidentiality or liability limits are stated in the excerpt.
termination_clause: 5.1 Subject to the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff and Rate Schedule FTS/ITS, this Agreement shall be effective as of the date of physical completion of and initial deliveries on Transporter's pipeline and shall continue for a primary term of ten years. Thereafter, this Agreement shall be effective month to month, until terminated by Transporter or Shipper upon the following written notice to the other specifying a termination date: sixty (60) days for interruptible transportation under Rate Schedule ITS and 180 days for firm transportation under Rate Schedule FTS.

summary: FAILED

This Collaborative Research, Development and Commercialization Agreement, dated June 8, 2018, sets up a partnership between Revolution Medicines (RevMed) and Sanofi to discover, develop and potentially market drug candidates that inhibit the cancer target SHP2. RevMed contributes its expertise in cancer biology and its proprietary know‑how on SHP2 inhibitors, while Sanofi provides pharmaceutical development, regulatory, and commercial capabilities. The parties agree to share licenses, jointly manage research programs, and cooperate on manufacturing and marketing of any resulting products. Key obligations include RevMed granting Sanofi rights to use its licensed know‑how and Sanofi committing to commercially reasonable efforts to develop and sell the products. Notable risks involve regulatory compliance, potential change‑of‑control events that could affect ownership or control, and the need to protect confidential information, though specific confidentiality or liability provisions are not included in the excerpt.

This Sponsorship Agreement, dated September 23, 1998, sets out a partnership between Excite, Inc. and Vitamin Shoppe Industries Inc. Excite agrees to promote the Client’s vitamin products as the preferred and dominant reseller on various sections of the Excite and WebCrawler sites, including health and shopping channels, by providing banner ads, links, and sponsorship placements. The Client must supply all graphics, text, keywords and URLs (the "Impression Material") at least 14 days before the scheduled launch date and cooperate in good‑faith to identify additional promotional opportunities. Risks include the Client’s failure to deliver materials on time, which could delay launch or cause Excite to use incomplete assets, and the fact that Excite’s performance estimates are not guaranteed, potentially limiting the expected exposure for the Client.

This agreement is a non‑disclosure and non‑competition contract between a franchisee and an individual employee (the Member) who will have access to the franchisor’s proprietary system. The Member must keep all confidential information secret during and after the franchise term, and may only use it to run the franchised restaurant. The Member also agrees not to work for, own, or assist any competing pizza‑restaurant business during the franchise term and for two years after termination within a three‑mile radius of any franchised location. Violations trigger injunctive relief, with the Member responsible for the franchisor’s court costs and reasonable attorney fees, creating a significant financial risk for non‑compliance.
confidentiality_clause: 1. Confidential Information. Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any confidential information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee. All information, knowledge and know-how relating to Franchisor, its business plans, Franchised Businesses, or the System ("Confidential Information") is deemed confidential, except for information that Member can demonstrate came to Member's attention by lawful means prior to disclosure to Member; or which, at the time of the disclosure to Member, had become a part of the public domain.

summary: This Strategic Alliance Agreement sets out a partnership between Gold Resource Corporation (GRC) and Hochschild Mining Holdings Ltd. (HOC). HOC receives an option to buy up to 4.33 million additional shares of GRC at $3.00 each, which must be exercised in writing before the option expires 80 days after closing. If exercised, GRC must allocate at least $5 million of the proceeds to exploration and $13 million to development, construction and working capital for the El Aguila project. HOC also has a right of first refusal to provide any extra equity financing before the mine begins production, on terms tied to the recent market price. HOC may buy GRC stock on the market or privately, but cannot own more than 40% of the outstanding shares for two years. Risks include missing the option deadline, forfeiting financing rights, and breaching the ownership cap, which could trigger loss of preferred rights or dilution.

This Distributor Agreement lets PreCheck Health Services act as a non‑exclusive distributor for Co‑Diagnostics’ qPCR COVID‑19 test kits in Romania. The Principal must supply the products, keep the right to market directly, and can change product lists or prices unilaterally. The Distributor must obtain all licenses, promote the products, and avoid selling competing items, while paying its own staff and sub‑distributors. The contract runs until March 18, 2021 but can be ended by either side with 30 days’ written notice; upon termination the Distributor can fulfill any orders already accepted. Risks include the Principal’s ability to alter prices or drop products without consent and the Distributor’s obligation to comply with all applicable laws without any liability limitation.
termination_clause: 10. This Agreement shall be in effect until March 18. 2021, unless sooner terminated by either party upon (30) days written notice, without cause. 11. In the event of termination, the Distributor shall be entitled to receive all orders accepted by the Principal prior to the date of termination and may sell the ordered Products in the Territory. Payment to be made upon shipment. 12. In the event of termination, neither party, their heirs nor successors shall issue any challenge whatsoever to contest the termination.

summary: This agreement makes PPG Shanghai the supplier of its Deltron and ACS automotive refinish products to NeoMedia Micro Paint Repair, which will act as the distributor in mainland China. PPG grants a non‑exclusive right to sell the products in the territory, while the distributor must meet sales targets, promote the products, provide customer service, and use PPG’s trademarks only with approval. PPG must supply products, provide marketing support, training, and rebates if targets are met. The distributor must pay for products up front, handle its own logistics, and avoid selling outside the territory. Risks include immediate termination if the distributor sells outside the territory and limited liability for PPG, which only promises product title and limited warranty, disallowing any other implied warranties.
termination_clause: If DISTRIBUTOR sells or distributes the Products outside the Territory, whether directly or indirectly through DISTRIBUTOR's cooperation or in conjunction with other third parties, or to customers outside the Territory without the prior knowledge and consent of PPG SHANGHAI, PPG SHANGHAI shall have the right to terminate with immediate effect this Agreement and any existing incentive arrangements between PPG SHANGHAI and DISTRIBUTOR, whether entered into before or after the commencement of the Agreement.
liability_clause: PPG SHANGHAI warrants only its title to the Products and that the Products will be as set forth in the warranty statement, if any, on the Products' labeling or in the absence of any such warranty statement that the Products will conform to PPG SHANGHAI's standard warranty when they are taken from PPG SHANGHAI's warehouse by DISTRIBUTOR or its transport agent. DISTRIBUTOR is not authorized to make warranties or representations on behalf of PPG SHANGHAI and shall make no such warranties or representations. THESE ARE THE ONLY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES THAT PPG SHANGHAI MAKES, AND ALL OTHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED WARRANTIES UNDER STATUTE OR ARISING OTHERWISE IN LAW FROM A COURSE OF DEALING OR USAGE OF TRADE, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ANY WARRANTY OF MERCHANTIBILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE OR USE, ARE DISCLAIMED BY PPG SHANGHAI. In the event that the Product fails to conform to the warranties herein given, DISTRIBUTOR's exclusive remedy and PPG SHANGHAI's sole responsibility is, at PPG SHANGHAI's option, limited to the replacement of such nonconforming Product at PPG SHANGHAI's...

summary: This Intellectual Property Agreement, dated Dec. 31, 2018, is between Armstrong Flooring (Seller), AFI Licensing (Licensing), and AHF Holding (Buyer) to handle the transfer and licensing of intellectual property that will be used after the Stock Purchase Agreement closes. Arizona (the Seller and Licensing) assigns all of its owned IP that is used exclusively in the Company’s business to the Company, and the Company assigns its own IP used in Arizona’s business back to Arizona. Both sides also grant each other perpetual, royalty‑free, non‑exclusive licenses for copyrights, know‑how, patents and certain trademarks, with limited, time‑bound licenses for other trademarks. Risks include the need to maintain quality control over trademark use, comply with the existing Trademark License Agreement, and ensure proper recordation of assignments; failure could jeopardize the validity of the transferred IP and expose the parties to reputational damage.

FAILED

This Content License Agreement gives Pacificap Entertainment a ten‑year exclusive, worldwide right to let The Henry Film and Entertainment Corporation use its nostalgic TV‑show library to create a wide range of media products. Pacificap must deliver the content in the requested digital formats and provide technical support, while Henry must pay a 10% share of net advertising, syndication and licensing revenue and guarantee at least $50,000 per year; failure to meet the minimum lets Pacificap cancel the deal. Both parties must keep each other's proprietary information confidential, and either side can terminate immediately for insolvency, bankruptcy, or a material breach not cured within 30 days. Risks include loss of revenue if the minimum isn’t met, potential termination without further liability, and the need to defend against third‑party IP claims under the indemnification provision.
termination_clause:
TERM AND TERMINATION

INITIAL TERM AND RENEWALS: This Agreement will become effective as of the last date of signature (Effective Date) and shall, unless sooner terminated as provided below or as otherwise agreed, remain effective for an initial term of 10 Years ... After the Initial Term, this Agreement will be automatically renewed for successive additional 3‑year periods ("Extension Terms"), unless otherwise terminated by either party by giving notice to the other party not less than sixty (60) days prior to the end of a Term.

TERMINATION FOR CAUSE: Notwithstanding the foregoing, this Agreement may be terminated by either party immediately upon notice if the other party: (w) becomes insolvent; (x) files a petition in bankruptcy; (y) makes an assignment for the benefit of its creditors; or (z) breach any of its obligations under this Agreement in any material respect, which breach is not remedied within thirty (30) days following written notice to such party.

EFFECT OF TERMINATION: Any termination shall be without any liability or obligation of the terminating party, other than with respect to any breach of this Agreement prior to termination. The provisions relating to property rights and confidentiality shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement. All revenue sharing ceases with the termination of this Agreement.
confidentiality_clause: PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT and THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION hereby acknowledge that each of them may have access to confidential and proprietary information, which relates to the other party's business (the "Confidential Information"). Such information shall be identified as confidential at the time of disclosure. Each party agrees to preserve and protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and not to disclose or use any applicable Confidential Information without the prior written consent of the other party; provided, however, that any party hereto may disclose to any other party or use any information which is: (i) already publicly known; (ii) discovered or created independently of any involvement with such party; (iii) otherwise learned through legitimate means other than from such party; or (iv) independently created by the receiving party without reference to the other party's confidential information. Moreover, any party hereto may disclose any Confidential Information hereunder to such party's agents, attorneys and other representatives or any court or competent jurisdiction or any other party empowered hereunder as reasonably required to resolve any dispute between the parties hereto. Both parties agree all aspects of this contract are confidential and shall not be disclosed to any third party.

summary: This Franchise Agreement lets the franchisee operate a hotel under the Homewood Suites by Hilton brand. The franchisor (Hilton) grants a non‑exclusive license to use the brand, its trademarks, manuals, and reservation systems, while the franchisee must build or refurbish the hotel to brand standards, complete required training, run the hotel according to the franchisor’s operational guidelines, and pay monthly program and royalty fees. The franchisor provides marketing support, inspections, and ongoing consultation. Risks include possible termination (see Section 14) if the franchisee fails to cure defaults, and liquidated or actual damages on termination. The franchisee also bears obligations to maintain records, allow audits, protect proprietary information, and comply with anti‑corruption and privacy laws, with indemnity obligations for breaches.

This addendum is a disclosure required by the New York Department of Law for a franchise agreement. It obligates the franchisor and its affiliates to disclose any pending or past litigation, criminal convictions, injunctive orders, or bankruptcy filings that could affect the franchise. The franchisor must confirm that neither it nor its predecessors, officers, or affiliates have been involved in serious legal actions, fraud, or bankruptcy within the specified time frames. The master franchisee signs the document to acknowledge receipt of these disclosures. The main risk for the franchisee is entering a relationship without full knowledge of hidden legal or financial problems, while the franchisor faces penalties for misrepresentation or omission of required information.

This amendment updates the 2014 Non‑Competition Agreement between Vivint Solar and Vivint, both affiliates of 313 Acquisition. The parties delete the original non‑competition provision (Section 2) and the original non‑solicitation provision (Section 4), replacing the latter with a new term that ties the agreement’s duration to the "Sales Term" defined in a separate Sales Dealer Agreement dated August 16, 2017. Except for these changes, the rest of the original non‑competition agreement remains in force. The amendment is governed by the Master Framework Agreement, which includes confidentiality and miscellaneous provisions. Risks include being bound by the original restrictive covenants that were not deleted, and the agreement’s continuation for the full sales term, potentially limiting future business activities for the affiliates.

This Development Agreement sets out a collaboration where Cargill will modify its yeast strain CB1 to produce succinic acid for Bioamber, while Bioamber funds the work and pays milestone fees. Cargill must perform the work with qualified staff, maintain permits, and is solely responsible for its employees' negligence. Bioamber must pay an upfront $250,000, annual FTE fees, and additional sums when milestones are reached; missed milestones trigger penalty payments or accelerated due amounts if Bioamber commercializes the strain. Both parties must keep all shared technical and business information confidential. Risks include Cargill’s liability limited to specified milestone penalties and Bioamber’s exposure to extra fees if milestones are delayed or not met.
confidentiality_clause:
4. Confidentiality. To carry out the Work Plan, Cargill may receive from, and provide to, Bioamber certain Confidential Information, as defined below. Such Confidential Information will be disclosed by one Party (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party (the "Receiving Party") on the following terms and conditions:

4.1 "Confidential Information" means all business, technical, and financial information related to the Work Plan, the terms and discussions relating to the Term Sheet executed by the parties on December 3, 2009 and December 4, 2009, and to any aspect of the business of each Party that is material to the Work Plan, including, without limitation, Licensed Tool Kit, products, product compositions, raw materials, specifications, formulae, equipment, business plans and strategies, customer lists, supplier lists, know-how, samples, drawings, pricing information and other financial information, inventions, ideas, research information, packaging, manufacturing processes, and other information, or its potential use, that is owned by or in possession of either Party. For purposes of clarity, the parties shall not disclose to each other any Confidential Information that is not material to the Work Plan, such as, by way of example and without limitation, processes and other information relating to post-fermentation activities. Confidential Information shall not include information that: (a) is in the public domain prior to disclosure by Disclosing Party; (b) becomes part of the public domain, by publication or otherwise, through no unauthorized act or omission by the Receiving Party; (c) is lawfully in the Receiving Party's possession prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (d) is independently developed by an employee(s) of the Receiving Party with no access to the disclosed Confidential Information.

4.2 The Receiving Party agrees to take and maintain proper and appropriate steps to protect Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party agrees to disclose the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party only to employees or agents of the Receiving Party who are directly involved with the Work Plan contemplated by this Development Agreement, and even then only to such extent as is necessary and essential to perform the Work Plan. The Receiving Party agrees to inform such employees and agents of the confidential nature of the information disclosed hereunder and to cause all such employees and agents to abide by the terms of this Development Agreement.

4.3 The Receiving Party shall not disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to any unauthorized party
liability_clause: 2.4.3 Provided that Cargill has respected its undertakings set out in Section 1.2 of this Development Agreement, no other damages shall accrue to Cargill for not achieving a given Milestone other than provided in Sections 2.4.1, 2.4.2, and 2.5.

summary: This agreement lets the Supplier produce merchandise that uses Playboy’s trademarks only at the direction of the Purchaser. The Supplier must not sell or ship any Playboy‑branded items without an order, must not claim ownership of the trademarks, and must limit production to the quantities and designs the Purchaser specifies. The Supplier must return or destroy all molds and designs when asked. Playboy is a third‑party beneficiary, and the contract supersedes any conflicting purchase‑order terms. The Supplier is required to indemnify and hold harmless Playboy and the Purchaser for any defects, creating a significant liability risk. The contract can only be changed or ended in writing signed by Playboy, and any breach could lead to fines, criminal penalties, or prosecution for trademark infringement.
termination_clause: This Contract may not be modified or terminated except in writing, and no claimed modification, termination or waiver shall be binding unless also signed by an authorized representative of Playboy.
liability_clause: Supplier will defend, indemnify and hold harmless Purchaser and Playboy and the distributors and dealers and the officers and employees of each of the foregoing against all liability whatsoever which may be incurred by them or any of them as a result of any alleged defects in material or workmanship in the merchandise covered by this Contract.

summary: This Web Site Hosting Agreement sets out i‑on’s duty to keep the Customer’s hosted website running 24/7, provide internet connectivity, storage, backups, and limited monthly admin support in exchange for a $450 monthly fee. The Customer must pay on time, supply all website content, handle user support, marketing, and security of admin accounts, and obtain i‑on’s approval for any custom scripts. Risks include service interruption if fees are late, data loss despite backups (i‑on is held harmless), and limited liability—i‑on’s exposure is capped at one month’s fees. Either party can end the contract with 30‑day notice, but the Customer must still pay all amounts due.
termination_clause:
The term of this Agreement for the Hosted Site shall commence upon April 1, 1999 and shall continue for a period of six (6) months, unless earlier terminated in accordance with provisions hereof. This Agreement shall automatically be renewed for one (1) or more one (1) month periods unless either the Customer or i‑on gives notice to the other party of its intention not to renew the Agreement, which notice must be given not less than fifteen (15) days before the end of the respective initial or renewal term.

Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause at any time effective upon thirty (30) days' written notice. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in this Agreement, no termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever shall relieve the Customer of the obligation to pay all amounts due to i‑on and to make such payments on a timely basis.
liability_clause: i‑on will not be liable under any circumstances for any lost profits or other consequential damages, even if i‑on has been advised as to the possibility of such damages. i‑on's liability for damages to the Customer for any cause whatsoever, regardless of the form of action, and whether in contract or in tort, including negligence, shall be limited to one (1) month's fees and the remaining portion of any prepaid fees.

summary: This amendment updates the original Wireless Content License Agreement between Fox Mobile Entertainment (on behalf of Twentieth Century Fox) and Glu Mobile. It adds a limited, non‑exclusive license for Glu Mobile to distribute specific Ice Age 2 audio and video wireless products in the U.S. and abroad. In exchange, Glu Mobile must pay Fox a royalty of ***** % of its gross receipts from each unit sold, and it cannot offset any prior Ice Age 2 guarantee against these sales. The amendment also clarifies that any revenue Fox earns from direct‑to‑consumer sales of these products belongs solely to Fox, and Glu Mobile has no claim to that income. All other terms of the original agreement remain unchanged. The main risk for Glu Mobile is the royalty obligation and the inability to recoup prior guarantees, while Fox retains full control over direct‑to‑consumer revenue.

This amendment updates the original manufacturing agreement between Stremick's Heritage Foods ("Heritage") and Premier Nutrition Corporation ("Premier"). The key change is that Section 2(c) is replaced with a new requirement that Premier must purchase a minimum annual order volume (MAOV) of a specified number of units for each twelve‑month contract period, starting July 1, 2018, and continuing each year thereafter, provided Heritage’s aseptic production facility is approved by Premier by January 1, 2019. The amendment also clarifies that all other terms of the original agreement stay unchanged. Risks for Premier include the obligation to meet the purchase minimums, which could be costly if demand falls short. Heritage’s risk is that the purchase commitment depends on facility approval; if the facility is not approved, the MAOV may not be enforceable. No new termination, confidentiality, or liability provisions are added.

This Hosting Agreement lets Customer use eGain's web‑based software and related hosting services. eGain must provide 24/7 live support, maintain the system, and ensure the software works as documented; if downtime occurs, eGain credits the Customer for service days. Customer must supply its own internet connection and equipment, keep passwords secure, use the system lawfully, and pay monthly fees on time, with late fees for overdue payments. Both parties must keep each other's confidential information private and return it upon termination. Liability is limited: eGain is not responsible for indirect or consequential damages and its total exposure is capped at the fees paid. The agreement runs for one year, auto‑renews, and can be ended with 30‑day notice or for material breach, after which services stop and any unpaid fees become due.
termination_clause:

In addition, either party may terminate this Agreement by giving to the other party written notice of such termination upon the other party's material breach of any material term (subject to the other party's right to cure within thirty (30) days after receipt of such notice), the other party's insolvency, or the institution of any bankruptcy or similar proceedings by or against the other party.

Except in the event of termination for Customer's breach, eGain shall provide Customer with an electronic copy of the final Reports (covering the month just prior to termination of this Agreement). eGain shall be entitled to retain a copy (whether electronic or otherwise) of the Online Messages and the Reports for its records and internal purposes and shall not disclose such Online Messages or Reports to any third party except as permitted under Section 4. Within fifteen (15) days of any termination of this Agreement, Customer shall pay to eGain all unpaid fees accrued prior to termination. confidentiality_clause: 8. Confidential Information. Each party agrees to keep confidential and to use only for purposes of performing (or as otherwise permitted under) this Agreement, any proprietary or confidential information of the other party disclosed pursuant to this Agreement which is marked as confidential or which would reasonably be considered of a confidential nature. The obligation of confidentiality shall not apply to information which is publicly available through authorized disclosure, is known by the receiving party at the time of disclosure as evidenced in writing, is rightfully obtained from a third party who has the right to disclose it, or which is required by law, government order or request to be disclosed. Upon any termination of this Agreement, each party shall return to the other party all confidential information of the other party, and all copies thereof, in the possession, custody or control of the party unless otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement.

summary: This excerpt outlines how California law affects a Master Franchise Agreement. It tells franchisees that state statutes protect their rights to terminate or not renew the franchise, and that any contract terms that conflict with those statutes will be overridden. The agreement also includes a termination‑upon‑bankruptcy clause, which might be invalid under federal bankruptcy rules, and a liquidated damages provision that could be unenforceable under California law. The document notes that non‑binding mediation will be held in New York, that New York law is designated to govern the contract (potentially contrary to California rules), and that franchisees must sign a general release to renew or transfer the franchise, which California law may void. Risks include unenforceable termination, mediation, and damages provisions, and possible penalties for violating state disclosure and waiver requirements.
termination_clause:
1. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043 provide rights to you concerning termination or non‑renewal of a franchise. If the Master Franchise Agreement contains provisions that are inconsistent with the law, the law will control.

2. The Master Franchise Agreement provides for termination upon bankruptcy. This provision may not be enforceable under Federal Bankruptcy Law (11 U.S.C.A. Sec. 101 et seq.).
liability_clause: 10. The Master Franchise Agreement contains a liquidated damages clause. Under California Civil Code Section 1671, certain liquidated damages clauses are unenforceable.

summary: This Trademark License Agreement lets Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (Licensee) use the "Morgan Stanley" brand for specific public‑filing and marketing purposes, while Morgan Stanley Investment Management Inc. (Licensor) retains ownership of the brand. Licensee must obtain prior written approval for each use, keep the brand’s quality standards, and comply with all laws. The Licensor can end the agreement at any time for convenience, for breach, or if the related investment advisory relationship ends, and the license runs forever unless terminated. Liability is limited: neither side is responsible for special or consequential damages, except for indemnification obligations. Risks include immediate termination for non‑compliance, loss of brand use, and the need to cease all activities upon termination.
termination_clause: Section 4. Termination. Section 4.1. Term. The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues in perpetuity, unless termination occurs pursuant to Sections 4.2 through 4.4. Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the standards and policies. Section 4.3. Termination for Breach. If either party materially breaches one or more of its obligations hereunder, the other party may terminate this Agreement, effective upon written notice, if the breaching party does not cure such breach within 15 days after written notice thereof (or any mutually agreed extension). Licensor may terminate this Agreement immediately, effective upon written notice, if (i) Licensee attempts to violate Section 8 or (ii) a sublicensee materially breaches its sublicense in a manner that harms the Brand, and (a) such sublicensee does not cure the same within 15 days after notice from Licensor or Licensee or (b) Licensee does not terminate such sublicense within 15 days after notice from Licensor. Section 4.4. Termination of Advisory Agreement. This Agreement shall terminate automatically without notice and immediately (a) if MS Capital Partners Adviser Inc. or another affiliate of Licensor is no longer acting as the investment adviser (any such entity, the "Advisor") to Licensee under the Investment Advisory Agreement, dated as of [·], 201[9] (as the same may be amended, modified or otherwise restated, the "Investment Advisory Agreement"), or a similar agreement, or (b) the Advisor is no longer an affiliate of Licensor. Further, Licensor may terminate this Agreement, effective upon written notice, at any time after 30 days from the date that Licensee notifies Licensor that the Investment Advisory Agreement has terminated or is not being renewed. Section 4.5. Effect of Termination; Survival. Upon termination of this Agreement for any reason, (a) Licensee shall immediately, except as required by applicable Law, (i) cease all use of the Permitted Activity; and (b) the parties shall cooperate so as to best preserve the value of the Brand. Sections 2, this Section 4.5, and Sections 6.2, 6.3, 7 and 9 shall survive termination of this Agreement.
liability_clause: Section 6.3. EXCEPT WITH RESPECT TO LICENSEE'S INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 7, NEITHER PARTY WILL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR SPECIAL, INDIRECT, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR INCIDENTAL DAMAGES (INCLUDING LOST PROFITS OR GOODWILL, BUSINESS INTERRUPTION AND THE LIKE) RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT, EVEN IF IT HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.

summary: This Corporate Sponsorship Agreement lets Freeze Tag, Inc. support the American Diabetes Association’s Get Fit Don’t Sit Day while the Association publicly acknowledges the sponsorship. Freeze Tag must use the Association’s trademarks only as approved, maintain product quality, and keep the Association’s tax‑exempt status safe. The Association must display the sponsor’s name and logos correctly and obtain its written consent for any use. Both parties must keep the agreement’s terms and any non‑public business information confidential. Risks include breach of trademark use rules, failure to maintain required liability insurance, and material breaches that can trigger termination with ten days’ notice. Each party is liable only for its own actions, and indemnifies the other for related claims.
termination_clause: Before expiration of the Term, either Party may terminate this Agreement upon: (i) any material breach of the Agreement by the other Party, if such breach is not remedied to the reasonable satisfaction of the non-breaching Party within ten (10) business days after written notice; (ii) ten (10) business days written notice to the other Party whenever the notifying Party in its sole discretion determines that the continuation of the Agreement will damage its reputation or good will; or (iii) written notice in the event one Party (a) becomes or is declared insolvent or bankrupt or is subject to the appointment of a trustee or receiver or any equivalent thereof, (b) is the subject of any proceeding related to its liquidation or insolvency (whether voluntary or involuntary) which is not dismissed within ninety (90) days, (c) makes an assignment for the benefit of creditors, or (d) is subject to any sale, lease or other transfer of all or substantially all of its assets to any entity; or (e) is subject to a change of control (whether by merger, stock transfer or otherwise), except in the case of an initial public offering.
confidentiality_clause: The provisions of this Agreement shall be maintained by the Parties as confidential during the Term and thereafter. In addition, any and all aspects of Company's business, including without limitation all non-public information or trade secrets directly or indirectly related thereto, that Association becomes exposed to during the Term, and extensions or renewals, of this Agreement shall be maintained as confidential, and shall not be further disclosed by Association, or used by Association for any purpose other than performing hereunder during the Term or thereafter. Company shall at all times retain full ownership in and to all information respecting its business, and shall be the sole and exclusive owner of all materials created by or for the Company hereunder, with the exception of the Association Marks.
liability_clause: Company and Association agree that each is responsible for its own business activities and for its action or inaction relating to the specific Program or Event activities under this Agreement. Company shall be responsible for securing any necessary release forms from participants in any Company activity not held at the Association's Program or Event activity.

summary: This Strategic Alliance Agreement sets up a partnership where Bravatek will act as a non‑exclusive lead‑finder for Fazync’s energy‑saving products in the government sector, while Fazync will promote Bravatek in its marketing and provide support on technical and administrative matters. Both parties must collaborate on joint product strategies, share marketing materials, and agree in writing on pricing for custom projects. Bravatek earns a lead‑finder fee of 10‑20% of project revenue, payable within 30 days of client payment. The agreement runs for 12 months, auto‑renews for another year unless either side gives 90‑day notice, and can be terminated with 90‑day written notice. Confidential information must be kept secret for five years after termination, and each party indemnifies the other for damages caused by its own negligence or breach.
termination_clause: This Agreement may also be terminated by either party upon ninety (90) days written notice.
confidentiality_clause: "Confidential information" shall mean any and all technical and non-technical information, documents and materials related to client projects of party and products, services and business of each of the parties. Fazync and Bravatek agree to maintain in strict confidence and not to disclose or disseminate, or to use for any purposes other than performance of the projects, the Confidential Information disclosed. The obligation of non-disclosure shall not apply to the following: a. Information at or after such time that is publicly available through no fault of either party b. Information at or after such time that is disclosed to either party by a third party entitled to disclose such information c. Information which is required by law to be disclosed to federal, state or local authorities.

summary: This Charter Affiliate Affiliation Agreement sets out the relationship between The TUBE Music Network (the "Network") and Tribune Broadcasting Company (the "Affiliate") for the broadcast of the Network's television service, "The TUBE," over the Affiliate's broadcast stations. The Affiliate must air the Service on its stations (including any it later acquires) starting on specified launch dates, use only over‑the‑air transmission (or approved MVPD carriage), and may insert a limited amount of locally produced programming. The Network retains full control over all content, scheduling, and any changes to the Service. The agreement runs from the effective date in March 2006 until March 31, 2011, with an automatic four‑year renewal unless the Affiliate gives six months’ notice before the end of the term. Risks include the Affiliate’s obligation to launch on time, to secure MVPD carriage where required, and to comply with FCC and licensing rules; failure to meet these obligations could lead to breach and termination, though specific penalties are not detailed in the excerpt.

This Strategic Alliance Agreement lets Northern Telecom (NTL) and Entrust Technologies (ETI) share and cross‑license each other's patents and technology. NTL receives a perpetual, worldwide right to use Entrust products and may contract for support services, paying $300,000 in 1997. ETI must grant NTL access to source code, enter reseller and source‑code license agreements, and pay fees tied to NTL's use of the technology. Both parties give each other royalty‑free patent licenses for products and services in their fields. Risks include a capped liability of $1 million for IP indemnity, an early‑termination clause that can end the reseller deal if ETI defaults or ceases to be NTL’s subsidiary, and no explicit confidentiality provisions in the excerpt.
termination_clause: Subject to early termination for material default, such Reseller Agreement shall expire either in three years or when ETI ceases to be a Subsidiary of NTL, whichever event occurs later.
liability_clause: ETI's liability to Nortel arising from or relating to the intellectual property indemnity set forth in the Enterprise License shall not exceed 50% (fifty percent) of the monies paid by Nortel thereunder to a maximum of U.S.$1,000,000 (one million U.S. dollars).

summary: This Collaboration Agreement sets out a partnership between MacroGenics and Green Cross to develop and, if approved, commercialize MacroGenics' anti‑HER2 antibody MGAH22 in South Korea. MacroGenics will grant Green Cross an exclusive license for all indications and pharmaceutical forms of the antibody in that territory, while Green Cross will fund development activities, obtain regulatory approvals, and handle commercialization, including sales, marketing, and post‑approval activities. Both parties must comply with applicable laws, good manufacturing and clinical practices, and share confidential information. Key risks include failure to secure regulatory approval, non‑compliance with cGMP/GCP standards, and potential loss of exclusivity if milestones are not met. The agreement also outlines cost allocations, such as commercial supply costs and fully‑burdened manufacturing costs, which could affect each party’s financial exposure.

contract_id: VAPOTHERM, INC. - Manufacturing and Supply Agreement
summary: This Manufacturing and Supply Agreement sets out a relationship where Vapotherm, a U.S. medical device maker, contracts Medica in Italy to produce specialized cartridges for its humidified gas therapy system. Vapotherm must give Medica regular forecasts and place purchase orders that meet minimum percentages of those forecasts, while Medica must keep a safety stock, meet strict quality specifications, and deliver on time. If deliveries are late or cartridges fail to meet specifications, Vapotherm can receive price reductions, demand replacements, or cancel orders. Payments are due within a set number of days, with interest penalties for late payment. The agreement also includes provisions for quality monitoring, inspections, recalls, and indemnification, but the excerpt does not contain the detailed termination, confidentiality, or liability language.

This agreement sets up an affiliate program where website owners (Affiliates) place links to software products on their sites. When a visitor clicks the link, purchases the software through element 5’s order system, the Affiliate earns a commission based on the sale price, up to 50% and subject to a minimum payout threshold. Affiliates must integrate the provided link correctly, keep their login credentials confidential, and display required disclosures about their partnership. element 5 handles order processing, payment collection, refunds, and passes the net proceeds (minus commission, service fees and taxes) to the Software Publisher. The Software Publisher configures commission rates and supplies advertising material, which the Affiliate may use but not alter without permission. Risks include loss of commission if links malfunction, delayed payments until the minimum threshold is met, and potential liability for incorrect pricing or content on the Affiliate’s site.

contract_id: HEMISPHERX - Sales, Marketing, Distribution, and Supply Agreement
summary: This Sales, Marketing, Distribution and Supply Agreement lets Hemispherx, the drug owner, grant Scien an exclusive right to sell and distribute its Interferon alfa‑n3 in the GCC after a successful MERS trial. Hemispherx must supply the product at a discounted transfer price and provide scientific support; Scien must create a business plan, obtain regulatory approvals, manage logistics, hold inventory, and pay for all marketing activities, reporting quarterly sales to Hemispherx. The contract runs for three years with automatic two‑year extensions, but can be ended for breach—such as missing purchase targets, failure to distribute, or insolvency. Risks include strict purchase milestones, payment within 75 days, and potential termination if either party becomes bankrupt or cannot fulfill obligations.
